natural_occurrence.description: Methyl cellulose is a semi-synthetic derivative of cellulose. While cellulose itself is natural (from plant cell walls), the methylation process creates a modified form not found in nature. It's essentially chemically modified wood pulp or cotton fiber.

manufacturing.description: Produced by treating cellulose (from wood pulp or cotton) with alkali, then reacting it with methyl chloride. This substitutes some hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the cellulose backbone with methyl (-OCH3) groups. The degree of substitution determines the properties - more methylation produces better water solubility and different gelling characteristics.

other_uses.cosmetics: Thickener and film-former in shampoos, lotions, and cosmetics.
other_uses.industrial: Construction (wallpaper paste, tile adhesive), ceramics, textiles, paint remover (thickens the gel).
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Bulk laxative (absorbs water in intestines), ophthalmic solutions, tablet binder.
other_uses.household: Wallpaper paste, paint strippers, personal lubricants.

eu.conditions: Widely permitted in most food categories; considered safe dietary fiber derivative.

commonly_found_in: Low-fat ice cream and frozen desserts
fun_facts: Methyl cellulose has a unique property - it gels when heated and becomes liquid when cooled, the opposite of gelatin! This is called 'reverse thermal gelation'
It's literally modified sawdust that's been chemically treated - you're eating chemically altered wood fiber
The same chemical in your gluten-free bread is also in wallpaper paste - both benefit from its adhesive and water-holding properties
Like plain cellulose, humans can't digest it, so it passes through as zero-calorie fiber
In hot frying oil, methyl cellulose in batter forms a gel layer that prevents oil absorption - making fried food less greasy
